clock_sync_ntp.py - reference impl. All numbers below feed CLOCK_SYNC_NTP.md.
Pure Python stdlib. rho = 1e-06

========================================================================
  SECTION A: Cristian's algorithm  (client/server, RTT/2 offset)
========================================================================
Setup: client asks a trusted server 'what time is it?'.
  T0 : client sends the request   (read on the CLIENT clock)
  T1 : server stamps the reply    (read on the SERVER clock)
  T2 : client receives the reply  (read on the CLIENT clock)
  RTT = T2 - T0  (measured entirely by the client).

Formulas:
  offset  theta    = T1 - (T0 + T2)/2     (ADD to client to match server)
  accuracy bound   = (T2 - T0)/2 = RTT/2  (|true - theta| <= this)

Ground truth for the demo: client clock == real time; server is +12.0 s ahead. TRUE offset = 12.0.

Case 1 (SYMMETRIC network, d_out=4.0, d_in=4.0):
  T0=0.0, T1=16.0, T2=8.0, RTT=8.0
  theta = T1 - (T0+T2)/2 = 16.0 - 4.0 = 12.0000
  accuracy bound = RTT/2 = 4.0000
  error = |true - theta| = 0.000000  (ZERO -> symmetric delay cancels out exactly)

Case 2 (ASYMMETRIC network, d_out=3.0, d_in=5.0):
  T0=0.0, T1=15.0, T2=8.0, RTT=8.0
  theta = T1 - (T0+T2)/2 = 15.0 - 4.0 = 11.0000
  accuracy bound = RTT/2 = 4.0000
  error = |true - theta| = 1.0000   == (d_in - d_out)/2 = 1.0000  (the asymmetry bias)
  true offset 12.0 in [theta-RTT/2, theta+RTT/2] = [7.0000, 15.0000]?  True

The punchline: Cristian's error is at most RTT/2. A 200 ms ping
=> you only ever know the time to +/- 100 ms. Want milliseconds of
accuracy? You need a sub-2-millisecond round trip, i.e. a LAN.

GOLD (pinned for clock_sync_ntp.html) - Case 2 scenario:
  T0=0, d_out=3, d_in=5, server_ahead=12 -> T1=15, T2=8
  theta  = 11.0     (must be exactly 11.0)
  accuracy = 4.0   (must be exactly 4.0)
  true_offset = 12.0 , error = 1.0  (== (d_in-d_out)/2)
[check] Cristian GOLD: theta=11.0, accuracy=4.0, bound holds:  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION B: Berkeley algorithm  (master averages, tells slaves to adjust)
========================================================================
No machine is the 'truth'. A master polls every clock, AVERAGES them,
and tells each machine how to nudge itself so they all meet in the middle.

  average = mean(all clocks, master included)
  adj_i   = average - clock_i    (+ => speed up, - => slow down)

| machine  | clock (s vs master) | average | adjustment = avg - clock |
|----------|---------------------|---------|--------------------------|
| master   |                +0.0 |    15.0 |                    +15.0 |
| slave A  |               +10.0 |    15.0 |                     +5.0 |
| slave B  |               +20.0 |    15.0 |                     -5.0 |
| slave C  |               +30.0 |    15.0 |                    -15.0 |

average = (0 + 10 + 20 + 30) / 4 = 15.0 s
After applying the adjustments every clock reads 15.0 s. They AGREE, even though none was right.

Weakness (why we need Marzullo next): one liar drags the average.
  If slave C jumps to +300 s, average becomes 82.5 s and the
  master would obediently slow itself by 82.5 s to match garbage.
  Fix: reject outliers BEFORE averaging (Marzullo, section_c).

[check] clean-case adjustments sum to 0: True  (they must, by construction)

========================================================================
  SECTION C: NTP strata + Marzullo's intersection algorithm
========================================================================
NTP builds a HIERARCHY. Stratum 0 = a reference clock (atomic/GPS);
stratum 1 is wired directly to stratum 0; stratum 2 syncs from stratum
1; and so on. Each hop ADDS error. Stratum 16 means 'unsynchronized'.

| stratum | what it is                          | typical error vs UTC |
|---------|-------------------------------------|----------------------|
| 0       | reference clock (atomic / GPS / radio) | < 1 us  (the truth)  |
| 1       | server directly attached to stratum 0 | ~few ms              |
| 2       | syncs from one or more stratum-1    | ~10s of ms           |
| 3       | syncs from stratum-2                | ~100 ms              |
| 4       | syncs from stratum-3                | ~hundreds of ms      |
| 16      | unsynchronized / unreachable        | no guarantee         |

Error accumulates DOWN the tree: a stratum-3 clock is wrong by
everything stratum 1 and 2 were wrong by, plus its own RTT/2.

Marzullo's algorithm (1984): given several servers, each returning
(offset, +/- error), keep only the LARGEST clique of intervals that all
overlap; discard the rest as outliers. This is NTP's outlier filter.

| server          | offset +/- error | interval [lo, hi] |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| server A        |  10.0 +/- 2.0    | [  8.0,  12.0]      |
| server B        |  11.0 +/- 2.0    | [  9.0,  13.0]      |
| server C        |  12.0 +/- 2.0    | [ 10.0,  14.0]      |
| server D (liar) |  25.0 +/- 2.0    | [ 23.0,  27.0]      |

Largest overlapping clique = {A, B, C} = 3 servers
  region of agreement = [10.0, 12.0], midpoint estimate = 11.00
  rejected as outliers = {D}

[check] Marzullo picks the 3 agreeing servers and rejects the liar: trusted=[0, 1, 2], region=(10.0, 12.0):  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION D: clock drift  (rho ~ 1e-6, worst-case divergence = 2*rho*t)
========================================================================
A quartz crystal drifts at rate rho. The textbook value is ~1 ppm,
so a single clock can be off by rho*t and two clocks can differ by
up to 2*rho*t. Once you SYNC, the clock IMMEDIATELY starts drifting
again - synchronization is not a state, it is a recurring payment.

rho = 1e-06  (1 ppm). Sanity: rho * 12 days = 1.0368 s  (~ '1 second per 12 days' per clock)

| elapsed       | rho * t   (1 clock vs truth) | 2*rho*t  (two clocks)   |
|---------------|------------------------------|-------------------------|
| 1.000 s       | 1.00 us (1e-06 s)            | 2.00 us (2e-06 s)       |
| 1.00 min      | 60.00 us (6e-05 s)           | 120.00 us (0.00012 s)   |
| 1.00 h        | 3.60 ms (0.0036 s)           | 7.20 ms (0.0072 s)      |
| 1.00 days     | 86.40 ms (0.0864 s)          | 172.80 ms (0.1728 s)    |
| 12.00 days    | 1.037 s (1.037 s)            | 2.074 s (2.074 s)       |
| 1.00 years    | 31.558 s (31.56 s)           | 1.05 min (63.12 s)      |

So even if you perfectly sync two machines, after ONE DAY they can
already differ by 0.1728 s, and after a year by 63.12 s.
Moral: you must RE-SYNC periodically (NTP polls every ~17 minutes by
default) just to hold the drift budget.

[check] 2*rho*t for t=1 day == 2 * 1e-6 * 86400 == 0.1728 s:  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION E: why you can NEVER fully trust a wall clock
========================================================================
Even with Cristian + Berkeley + NTP + Marzullo, wall-clock timestamps
are unsafe to treat as monotonic facts. Four classic ways they bite:

(1) LEAP SECONDS. The IERS occasionally inserts a 61st second into a
    UTC minute: 23:59:59 -> 23:59:60 -> 00:00:00. A naive clock either
    repeats 23:59:59 (so two distinct instants share a timestamp) or
    smears it. POSIX pretends all days are 86400 s, so the leap second
    is literally undefined behavior at the OS layer.

(2) NTP STEP ADJUSTMENTS. If ntpd computes an offset larger than the
    'slew' threshold (~128 ms), it STEPS the clock - an instantaneous
    jump that can be BACKWARD. Code that does `end - start` on wall-
    clock readings can get a NEGATIVE duration.

    demo: real work took 0.3 s around a -0.5 s NTP step:
          duration = end - start = 999.8 - 1000.0 = -0.200 s   (NEGATIVE!)

(3) VM MIGRATION. A live-migration pauses the guest for the memory-
    copy, then resumes. The guest wall clock JUMPS FORWARD by the
    pause (seconds to minutes) and the TSC may reset. Any timeout or
    lease based on wall time can fire spuriously.

(4) GOOGLE'S SMEAR (the fix for leap seconds). Instead of a 1 s jump,
    slow/speed the clock slightly over 24 h so the leap second slides
    through invisibly:
      smear rate = 1 s / 86400 s = 1.157407e-05 s/s = 11.57 us/s = 11.57 ppm
    Over 24 h the clock is at most ~0.5 s off UTC, but it NEVER jumps.
    The price: during the window your clock disagrees with unsmeared
    servers by up to half a second.

TAKEAWAY: never use wall-clock time to measure durations or order
events. Use a MONOTONIC clock (CLOCK_MONOTONIC / performance.now())
for elapsed time, and logical clocks / HLC for event ordering.

[check] smear rate = 1.157407e-05 s/s ~= 11.57 ppm:  OK

========================================================================
  DONE - all sections printed
========================================================================
